coredata.dc:title: Bismuth oxide self-standing anodes with concomitant carbon dots welded graphene layer for enhanced performance supercapacitor-battery hybrid devices
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenqiang
Xiao, Yi
Li, Xingwei
Cheng, Qilin
Wang, Gengchao
coredata.dc:description: To meet the energy storage demand of developing electronic information technology, the supercapacitor-battery hybrid devices based on Bi2O3 have attracted much attention due to their superior energy storage characteristics. However, the poor cycling stability limits its application. Herein, through the nano-welding technique induced by the microwave thermal effect of graphene, the carbon nanotube film-supported Bi2O3 electrode with a graphene/carbon dots encapsulation structure is fabricated. The as-prepared composite electrode delivers a remarkable capacity of 1.90 mAh cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2 as well as high rate performance (1.57 mAh cm−2 at 100 mA cm−2). Assembled device with above composite electrode as anode and the carbon nanotube loaded nickel cobalt as cathode exhibits a maximum energy density of 589.3 μWh cm−2 (98.2 Wh kg−1) at power density of 0.8 mW cm−2 and maintain 288.3 μWh cm−2 (48.1 Wh kg−1) at 57.1 mW cm−2. Most notably, by virtue of the flexible confinement of graphene, the device exhibits impressive cycle stability (80.1% capacity retention after 8000 cycles, ∼4.5 times than that of a device assembled with a raw anode). This work opens a new pathway to the design and fabrication for high energy density and durable cyclic stability energy storage devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth oxide
Graphene
Self-standing electrode
Nano-weld
Supercapacitor-battery hybrid devices

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanotubes loaded bismuth oxide electrode
Synthesis of graphene in-situ nano-welded bismuth oxide electrode
Synthesis of calcined carbon coated bismuth-based electrode

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FESEM image of CNT@Bi2O3. (b and c) Low- and high-magnification HRTEM images of CNT@Bi2O3. The inset is SAED pattern. (d) FESEM image of CNT@Bi2O3-G. The inset is High magnification FESEM image. (e and f) Low- and HRTEM images of CNT@Bi2O3-G (The outline shows the direction of observation).

(a) XRD pattern of CNT@Bi2O3 (b) XRD patterns of CNT@Bi2O3-G and CNT@Bi2O3-C. (c) Raman spectrum of CNT@Bi2O3-G. (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f for three electrodes. (e and f) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s and O 1 s for CNT@Bi2O3-G.

(a) CV curves of CNT@Bi2O3-G at different scan rates. (b) CV curves of three anodes at 5 mV s−1. (c) GCD curves of CNT@Bi2O3-G at different various current densities. (d) GCD curves of three electrodes at 5 mA cm−2. (e) Rate performance of three anodes. (f) EIS of three electrodes. The inset is the magnification image of high-mid frequency region.

(a) CV curves under various potential windows at 5 mV s−1, (b) CV curves of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G at different scan rates and (c) GCD curves at different various current densities of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G. (d) EIS. Inset is the magnification image of high-mid frequency region and (e) Ragone plots of three devices. (f) Cycle stability curves of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G and CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3.

(a) Schematic for the mechanism of graphene in-situ nano-welded structure. (b) CV curves at 5 mV s−1 and (c) EIS of three devices after 8000 charge-discharge cycles. (d–f) FE-SEM images of CNT@Bi2O3, CNT@Bi2O3-G and CNT@Bi2O3-C extracted from the devices after 8000 cycles (scale bars: 100 nm).
Schematic illustration for the formation process for CNT@Bi2O3-G.

head.ce:title: Bismuth oxide self-standing anodes with concomitant carbon dots welded graphene layer for enhanced performance supercapacitor-battery hybrid devices

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials, Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Laboratory of Advanced Polymeric Materials
Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education
School of Materials Science and Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To meet the energy storage demand of developing electronic information technology, the supercapacitor-battery hybrid devices based on Bi2O3 have attracted much attention due to their superior energy storage characteristics. However, the poor cycling stability limits its application. Herein, through the nano-welding technique induced by the microwave thermal effect of graphene, the carbon nanotube film-supported Bi2O3 electrode with a graphene/carbon dots encapsulation structure is fabricated. The as-prepared composite electrode delivers a remarkable capacity of 1.90 mAh cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2 as well as high rate performance (1.57 mAh cm−2 at 100 mA cm−2). Assembled device with above composite electrode as anode and the carbon nanotube loaded nickel cobalt as cathode exhibits a maximum energy density of 589.3 μWh cm−2 (98.2 Wh kg−1) at power density of 0.8 mW cm−2 and maintain 288.3 μWh cm−2 (48.1 Wh kg−1) at 57.1 mW cm−2. Most notably, by virtue of the flexible confinement of graphene, the device exhibits impressive cycle stability (80.1% capacity retention after 8000 cycles, ∼4.5 times than that of a device assembled with a raw anode). This work opens a new pathway to the design and fabrication for high energy density and durable cyclic stability energy storage devices.

ce:section.ce:para: The promising future and the existing deficiency of virtual reality (VR) gears, intelligent wears, electric vehicles and renewable energy storage have drawn a tremendous attention. The requirement for novel and more outstanding energy storage devices emerges, which needs devices with enhanced electrical properties, more electric energy, higher power, longer life-span and improved customization [1–3]. Among series of potential qualified candidates, aqueous supercapacitor, due to its high power density, excellent cycle stability and low maintenance cost, is industrially and academically popular in the field of new energy resource research [4–6]. Although many researchers have made unremitting efforts on designing better supercapacitor electrodes, the most serious problem that limits the application of aqueous supercapacitors is still the insufficient energy density [7–9]. Besides the micro/nano structure design of electrode materials, assembling supercapacitor-battery hybrid devices (SBHs) is an effective path to combine the advantages of both supercapacitors (rate, cycle life) and advanced batteries (energy density), which also can utilize the different potential windows of the two electrodes to increase the maximum operation voltage (1.6–2.0 V) [8–12].

According to the charge balance equation of
q +
=
q -
, to maximize the energy density of the device, the specific capacity of the cathode and the anode should be equal [13,14]. However, compared to the cathode materials with excellent performance, like MnOx, NiO, CoOx, NiCo2O4, and so on (theoretical specific capacity >200 mAh g−1 in aqueous electrolytes) [15–30], the research of advanced anode materials is rare. The frequently-used anodes of bare carbon material confined to the electric double layer energy storage mechanism (typical specific capacity <20 mAh g−1), are obviously unable to meet the needs of high energy storage density. Hence, some metal oxides such as Fe2O3, Fe3O4, and Bi2O3, owing to their high theoretical specific capacity (theoretical specific capacity >300 mAh g−1 in aqueous electrolytes) and relatively negative charge-discharge potential window, have shown the potential as the anodes [31–35]. Among them, Bi2O3 has attracted attention because of the nature of non-toxicity, easy fabrication and inexpensive [36–38]. Each Bi atom can exhibit a reversible redox reaction with three electrons transfer, which endows high theoretical specific capacities of 345 mAh g−1. In addition, the oxygen vacancy inherent in crystalline lattice of Bi2O3 endows its significant fast oxide ion diffusion, which benefits to the fast Faradic process of this conversion electrode material [39,40]. Lin et al. prepared Bi2O3-decorated electrospun carbon nanofibers which show a specific capacity of ∼0.15 mAh cm−2 at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 and outstanding flexibility [36]. Yuan et al. further introduced oxygen vacancies into Bi2O3 and obtained a specific capacity as high as ∼315.8 mAh g−1, achieving 83% of the theoretical value [37]. However, the charging-discharging process of bismuth-based anodes involves the formation of bismuth metal, which leads to dendritic segregation due to the uneven current density distribution. In addition, the repeated volume changes caused by the density difference of charge and discharge products will separate the active substance from the conductive network. The above problems lead to poor cycling stability of bismuth-based anodes.

In order to solve the problem of poor stability, some valuable research works have been carried out [41,42]. Lu et al. synthesized the single-crystalline bismuth on carbon cloth and assembled a NiCo2O4//Bi battery with 89% capacity retention after 1000 cycles [41]. Nevertheless, the insufficient active material loading limits the practical application of this process. Besides, carbonizing monomer or polymer to form the coating layer, can not only stabilize the structure but also reduce the impedance, has been explored on stabilizing bismuth-based electrodes [42]. However, the traditional carbonization process experienced long-time high temperature condition, which leads to the inevitable composition changes and structural destruction of well-designed nanomaterials. Further, the agglomerate structure and limited expansibility makes amorphous carbon layer difficult to adapt to the volume change of active materials and the poor affinity with hydrated ions of carbon hinders the penetration of aqueous electrolytes into the electrode. These problems of ordinary carbon layer make the specific capacity and rate performance of the device fail to reach the ideal level. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a high modulus, soft and thin carbon coating technology for stabilizing the structure and improving electrochemical performance of the bismuth-based anode. However, relevant researches have not been reported.

Graphene is a two-dimensional sp2 hybrid carbon atom layer with high conductivity, high modulus, and excellent intrinsic flexibility [43,44]. It is very popular to combine metal-based active materials with graphene to increase specific capacity and rate performance [10,13,32,37]. However, most of the works reported are simply to load the active material onto graphene, which has limited effectiveness in preventing the abscission of the active material due to poor binding. It can be deduced that a graphene encapsulation structure can not only improve the conductivity but also inhibit the loss of capacity. Herein, based on the understanding of the dielectric loss capability of graphene, we developed a novel graphene in-situ nano-welded technique for well-designed Bi2O3 nanoflakes loaded on flexible carbon nanotubes films (CNT@Bi2O3-G). Simply speaking, the heat generated by microwave absorption of graphene will carbonize the carbon source into carbon dots (CDs) used as the solder, so that the graphene is firmly welded on the active materials. The above process is rapid and minimizes damage to nanostructures. With this flexible adaptive graphene layer, the CNT@Bi2O3-G exhibits appreciable specific capacity (1.90 mAh cm−2/297.0 mAh g−1 at 1 mA cm−2) and excellent rate performance (82.6% retain at 100 mA cm−2). An SBH was further assembled with CNT@Bi2O3-G as anode, the carbon nanotube loaded nickel cobalt electrode (CNT@NiCo2O4) as cathode and KOH aqueous solution as electrolyte. The device with a potential window of 1.6 V reveals a maximum energy density of 589.3 μWh cm−2/98.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 0.8 mW cm−2. Most notably, compared with a device assembled with a raw anode, the 8000th cycle stability is increased by ∼4.5 times (capacity retain of 80.1%). These remarkable performances also unveil the prospect of our strategy promotion for applications with similar problems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanotubes loaded bismuth oxide electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (Sinophram Chemical Reagent Co.) is dissolved in a mixture of ethylene glycol/acetone with a volume ratio of 1/2 under vigorous agitation for 24 h, resulting in a concentration of 0.07 M, which was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The CNT film (SCNC-C, thickness 10–15 μm, Suzhou Jiedi Nano Science and Technology Co., Ltd.) was cut into piece, and then was immersed into above solution and kept at 160 °C for 6 h. After reaction, the loaded film was ultrasonically washed with ethanol, and dried at 105 °C, the as-prepared sample was labelled as CNT@Bi2O3. The typical area load mass of Bi2O3 is ∼5.5 mg cm−2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene in-situ nano-welded bismuth oxide electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The easily dispersed graphene was synthesized from natural graphite by a modified Hummers method and subsequent 60Co γ-ray irradiation reduction as our previous report [45]. The CNT@Bi2O3 film was immersed into a mixture of 0.25 g above graphene and 0.05 g glucose in 100 ml deionized water. After immersed for 24 h, the film was dried at 105 °C. Subsequently, the electrodes were transferred into a round flask with a suction outlet, vacuumed for 30 min and turn off the piston. Finally, the above sealed flask was irradiated by microwave for ∼5 s in a household microwave oven (Midea, 800 W), and the graphene in-situ nano-welded bismuth oxide electrode (CNT@Bi2O3-G) was obtained. For comparison, the control sample (CNT@Bi2O3-G(0)) was prepared through the similar procedure above without the presence of glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of calcined carbon coated bismuth-based electrode
ce:section.ce:para: As a contrast, the CNT@Bi2O3 film was immersed into 100 ml aqueous solution with 0.3 g glucose for 24 h. Then the CNT@Bi2O3 film was dried at 105 °C and carbonization at 700 °C for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/ min. the as-prepared sample was labelled as CNT@Bi2O3-C.

The morphologies of the samples were observed by field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100). The Raman spectra were collected using an invia reflex laser micro-Raman spectrometer with a wavelength of 514 nm. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were performed in a Rigaku D/Max 2550 VB/PC X-ray diffractometer using Cu (Kα) radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The elemental content was investigated by an energy-dispersive spectrometer (EDS, QUANTAX 400-30).

Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed on a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. And the cycling stability was measured on a program testing system (LAND CT2001A). For the three-electrode mode measurement, the prepared film electrode was used directly as working electrode with a Hg/HgO reference electrode and a Pt counter electrode. The electrochemical performance of the hybrid devices was measured under a two-electrode mode. According to charge balance, the self-standing bismuth-based anodes and CNT@NiCo2O4 cathode (the synthesis process is in Supporting Information) with a specific area ratio (details in the results and discussion) were sandwiched with a modified hydrophilic polypropylene separator [PPAT-CN1(2), Shanghai Shilong Hi-Tech Corp., Ltd]. The electrolyte was 6 M KOH aqueous solution. The scheme of the supercapacitor-battery hybrid device is shown in Fig. S1. For the specific area capacity, it can be calculated from charge/discharge curve according to the equation of Capacity = Is Δt/3600, where Is is the specific area discharge current, Δt is the discharge time (the corresponding mass specific capacity base on total electrode mass). The volumetric energy density (E) and power density (P) are calculated followed equations of E =
I s
∫ V
t
d t
and P = E/Δt.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the CNT@Bi2O3-G electrode was fabricated by loading, coating, and graphene in-situ nano-weld. The synthesis process starts with a solvothermal deposition, which causes the Bi2O3 nanoflakes to be deposited on carbon nanotubes of CNT films. Subsequently, the as-prepared CNT@Bi2O3 was immersed in a mixture containing well-dispersed graphene and glucose. Due to the presence of hydroxyl groups onto the surface of nanomaterials prepared by solvothermal and the oxygen-contain groups of the irradiated reduced graphene and glucose, the graphene and glucose will be self-assembled on the surface of Bi2O3 nanoflakes to form the precursor coating during the immersion process. It is well known that conductive materials can absorb electric field components of microwave and convert them into heat. By microwave irradiation in vacuum, graphene acts as a microwave absorbent to induce glucose carbonization, and the resulting CDs are connected with graphene and Bi2O3 by conjugate interaction and oxygen bridge (C-O-Bi), respectively.

Fig. S2a and b show the bare CNT film formed by nanotubes with a diameter of ca. 10 nm and Bi2O3 grown on the nanotubes. As shown on Fig. 1 a, the Bi2O3 nanoflakes with several nanometers of thickness were radially grown on the single carbon nanotube forming a coaxial heterostructure with a diameter of ca. 300 nm via solvothermal treatment. Fig. 1b shows the rim of CNT@Bi2O3, the bar-shaped shadow reflects the thickness of the Bi2O3 nanoflakes of ca. 3 nm. Clear lattice fringes with interspacing of 0.29 nm can be observed in the HRTEM image of Fig. 1c, which correspond to the (2 0 0) planes of δ-Bi2O3. The dispersion ring of select area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern confirms the presence of massive and disordered Bi2O3 nanocrystals.

After the construction of the graphene in-situ nano-welded layer, the low magnification image of Fig. S2c exhibits obvious coating on CNT@Bi2O3 without blocking the porous structure of the CNT film and maintain excellent flexibility of the electrode (inset of Fig. S2c). From Fig. 1d, it can be clearly observed that the wrinkle graphene is attached to the surface of single beam of CNT@Bi2O3, which are beneficial to improve the electrons transmission in the region far away from the central CNT axis of the nanoflakes. As a contrast, the CNT@Bi2O3-G(0) shows the serious aggregation of active materials (Fig. S3), which may be due to the carbothermal reaction initiated by the microwave directly melts the active material [46]. The inset exhibits the distributed CDs under the transparent graphene layer, which derive from the carbonization of glucose and play a role of the welded joint between graphene and Bi2O3. The Fig. 1e and Fig. S4 further confirm the morphology of graphene welded on CNT@Bi2O3 and the distribution of CDs. By construction a structure of CDs loaded on graphene, the identity and source of CDs have been further confirmed (Fig. S5). From the edge of the rolled graphene in Fig. 1f, it can be seen that the graphene has 1–2 layers. Moreover, the morphology of an ordinary carbon coated electrode (CNT@Bi2O3-C) is shown in Fig. S6. From Fig. S6a, the diameter of the single beam is decreased and the nanoflakes are replaced by smooth surface. From Fig. S6b, the nanoflakes are agglomerated into larger particles and surrounded by partially crystallized carbon layer with thickness of several nanometers. The collapse of nanoflakes structure obviously hinders the transmission of ions.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern in Fig. 2 a of CNT@Bi2O3 can be well assigned to the cubic phase of δ-Bi2O3 (JCPDS 74–1373). All the diffraction peaks are broadened relative to the standard pattern, which indicates that the as-prepared Bi2O3 has small crystalline size [33]. From Fig. 2b, after microwave treatment, the diffraction peak position of the (1 1 1) crystal plane shifts slightly toward smaller 2θ direction, which causes the crystal form to exhibit a tendency to transform to α-Bi2O3 (JCPDS 74–1374). However, the calcined carbon coating process badly reduces Bi2O3 to bismuth (JCPDS 85-1329), and the sharper diffraction peaks illustrate the serious agglomeration of nanostructure. Raman spectrum of CNT@Bi2O3-G (Fig. 2b) include D (∼1355 cm−1), G (∼1580 cm−1), D’ (∼1620 cm−1) and 2D (∼2670 cm−1) bands, respectively. The intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is ∼0.39, and the small ID/IG reflects a high degree of graphene reduction and glucose carbonization. The appearance of the D’ band indicate the existence of the defective edges of graphene [47]. Most notably, the 2D band shows the regular Lorentzien peak, which validate that the great majority of graphene is few-layered structure [48]. It can be inferred that the nano-welded graphene layer has excellent conductivity and flexibility, which is expected to greatly improve the rate performance and cycling stability of bismuth oxide. By comparison, Raman spectrum of CNT@Bi2O3-C with ID/IG of ∼0.83 (Fig. S7), show a typical amorphous carbon structure.

To further determine the valence state information of as-prepared materials and figure out the influence of microwave treatment process on the composition, XPS measurements were performed. The existence of C, O and Bi elements are unambiguously indicated on the full spectrum in Fig. S8. The spin splitting of Bi 4f orbital in Fig. 2d is divided into Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2. Two peaks of CNT@Bi2O3-G at 158.9 eV and 164.2 eV are similar to that of CNT@Bi2O3, indicating that microwave treatment does not change the composition of the active material. But for CNT@Bi2O3-C, two peaks at 157.3 eV and 162.6 eV corresponding to Bi0 appear after the calcination process, which correspond to the dense surface morphology and coincide with the XRD characterization [37,41]. For the C1s spectrum of CNT@Bi2O3-G, the highly intensive peak associated with the binding energy (∼284.8 eV) of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, and three weak peaks at ∼285.3 eV, ∼286.7 eV, and ∼289.3 eV associated with COH, CO, and OCO, respectively (Fig. 2e) [49]. These oxidized carbon atoms can be attributed to incomplete reduction of graphene and formation of CDs [50]. Fig. 2f shows the O 1 s spectrum deconvoluted into four peaks. In addition to the O 1 s in Bi2O3 of 530.4 eV, carbonyl of 531.6 eV and hydroxyl or epoxy of 533.6 eV, respectively, there is also a peak at 530.9 eV due to the possible formation of oxygen-bridge (C-O-Bi) [51]. Therefore, we speculate that CDs may anchor graphene onto bismuth oxide via chemical bonding.

The electrochemical performance of CNT@Bi2O3, CNT@Bi2O3-G, and CNT@Bi2O3-C was systematically studied in a three-electrode system with 6 M KOH solution. Fig. 3 a presents the CV profiles of CNT@Bi2O3-G at different scan rates. A couple of well-defined characteristic redox peaks distinctly identified in each CV profile, implying the highly reversible redox reaction and following Faradaic reactions by equation given below: Bi2O3 + 3H2O + 6e − ↔ 2Bi + 6OH− [32]. Fig. 3b compared the CV profiles of the above three electrodes at the scanning rate of 20 mV s−1. CNT@Bi2O3-G shows the largest encirclement area and a smaller degree of polarization, which is attributed to the confinement of nano-welded graphene/CDs not only shorten the path of electronic conduction but also maintained the migration channel of ions between nanoflakes. The GCD curves of CNT@Bi2O3-G at different current densities are shown in Fig. 3c, exhibiting stable platforms at ∼−0.57 V and ∼−0.53 V, which exhibits much better energy storage characteristics than CNT@Bi2O3-G(0) (Fig. S9). Fig. 3d compared the GCD curves of these three electrodes under the current density of 5 mA cm−2. Quite evidently, ordinary carbon-coated electrode exhibited a shorter charge-discharge plateau than the raw electrode and the graphene encapsulated electrode, suggesting that agglomeration of active materials during calcination leads to a decrease in utilization. The rate performance calculated from GCD data of the three electrodes is plotted in Fig. 3e. Apparently, CNT@Bi2O3-G combines high area specific capacity with excellent rate performance (1.90 mAh cm−2/297.0 mAh g−1 at 1 mA cm−2 and retain 82.6% at 100 mA cm−2). In contrast, CNT@Bi2O3 and CNT@Bi2O3-C exhibit significantly inferior electrochemical properties due to poor electron conductivity or difficult ion penetration (for the former, 1.92 mAh cm−2/299.9 mAh g−1 at 1 mA cm−2 and retain 60.6% at 100 mA cm−2; for the latter, 1.43 mAh cm−2/223.8 mAh g−1 at 1 mA cm−2 and retain 63.4% at 100 mA cm−2).

The difference in electrochemical performance of the three electrodes can be reflected by the composition of the impedance (Fig. 3f). Fig. S10 shows the equivalent circuit of the bismuth-based electrode to simulate the EIS results [52]. The Table S1 shows the fitting results of the equivalent series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of each electrode. It can be concluded that both ordinary carbon-coated and encapsulated graphene can significantly reduce the Rs (from 1.75 Ω of CNT@Bi2O3 to 1.34 Ω of CNT@Bi2O3-C and 1.28 Ω of CNT@Bi2O3-G). Prominently, for CNT@Bi2O3-G, the Rct that reflected by the semicircular diameter at the high frequency region, decreased from 0.34 Ω of CNT@Bi2O3 to 0.25 Ω. This is probably due to the existence of CDs, which improves wettability and accelerates the interface process. Moreover, for CNT@Bi2O3-G, the steepness of the straight line in low frequency region is similar to that of CNT@Bi2O3, indicating that graphene encapsulation does not hinder ion diffusion, which can be attributed to the porosity formed by the gas escaped during microwave irradiation. In contrast, the obvious tilt of CNT@Bi2O3-C in low frequency region confirms the formation of dense carbon layer by calcination. As a result, the CNT@Bi2O3-G electrode possesses a higher capacity and better rate performance.

To further investigate the advantages and feasibility of the graphene in-situ nano-welded technology for electrochemical energy storage, three SBHs were assembled by using above Bi2O3-based materials as anodes and CNT films supported NiCo2O4 (CNT@NiCo2O4) as cathodes (Fig. S11) with 6 M KOH electrolyte (defined as CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3, CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G, and CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-C, respectively). The electrochemical measurements described of Fig. S12 in Supporting Information indicate that CNT@NiCo2O4 cathode with typical pseudocapacitance characteristics are matched with those Bi2O3-based anodes [53]. Based on the specific capacity data of anode and cathode, the area ratio between the cathode and anode is optimized (CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3 and CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G, the ratio is 1.2:1; CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-C, the ratio is 0.85:1). The CV curves of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G in Fig. 4 a show that by the upper potential limit from 1.2 to 1.6 V, a couple of redox peaks appear gradually and more capacity can be utilized with the enlarged area. But, further increasing the potential to 1.8 V, the curve becomes distorted arising from the water decomposition. Thus, 0–1.6 V is chosen as the safe potential window for these SBHs, which is exactly the summation of the individual potential limit of anode and cathode. The CV profiles of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G at different scan rates are presented in Fig. 4b. Even if the scanning rate is increased from 1 mV s−1 to 50 mV s−1, well-defined redox couples are symmetric at 0.83 V with gradually widening potential differences, suggesting the excellent coulombic efficiency. From the asymmetric CV profiles of the other two devices (Fig. S13), the worse electrochemical performance is implied. Additionally, the typical GCD curves of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G at different current densities, ranging from 1 mA cm−2 to 100 mA cm−2, are shown in Fig. 4c. Corresponding to the CV profiles, a pair of slightly inclined platforms symmetrical to 0.83 V can be observed. The impedance of the device is further contrasted with the two control devices in Fig. 4d. The smaller ESR of 2.0 Ω and semicircle diameter of 1.48 Ω in the high-frequency region show the lower charge resistance. The straight line with an angle of ∼75° in the low-frequency region is between the 45° of the batteries and the 90° of the capacitors, indicating faster electrochemical kinetic behavior than batteries.

The specific capacity of three devices at different current density can be found in Fig. S14. It can be seen that CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G has the best electrochemical performance (specific capacity of 0.73 mAh cm−2 at 1 mA cm−2 and retain 65.8% at 50 mA cm−2). The Ragone plots in Fig. 4e illustrate an extremely high energy density of 589.3 μWh cm−2/98.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 0.8 mW cm−2 and 288.3 μWh cm−2/48.1 Wh kg−1 at 57.1 mW cm−2. Such a remarkable energy storage performance is much better than the other two devices assembled and the recently reported SBHs, supercapacitors and aqueous batteries (Table S2), such as 9.38 Wh kg−1 of RGO/MnOx@HCNs [13], 28.06 Wh kg−1 of rGO/PANI [21], 43.4 μWh cm−2/11.3 Wh kg−1 of MnO2//Bi2O3 [35], 78 Wh kg−1 of LiMn2O4//Bi2O3 [36], 449 μWh cm−2 of Co3O4/GN/BC//r-Bi2O3/GN/BC [37], 25.1 Wh kg−1 of NiCo2O4//Bi2O3 [38], 85.8 Wh kg−1 of NiCo2O4//Bi [41], 92 Wh kg−1 of Ni(OH)2//(BiO)4CO3(OH)2 [54], 43.75 Wh kg−1 of AC//NiO-AC NFs [55], 14.4 Wh kg−1 of Ni-G-CNFs@PANI [56], 95.8 Wh kg−1 of Ni(OH)2@Co9S8//FeS2/GNS [57], and 61.7 Wh kg−1 of NiCo2S4//rGO/Fe2O3 [58]. The cycling stabilities of these three devices are further examined at 10 mA cm−2 for 8000 cycles and shown in Fig. 4f. It can be seen that the capacity retention of the device with raw anode fall sharply below 40% in initial 2000 cycles and finally stay at ∼15%, presumably because the active materials are irreversibly detached from the carbon nanotubes. In vivid contrast, the capacity retention of the device with CNT@Bi2O3-G shows a gentle decline and the 8000th cycle stability increases by ∼4.5 times (from 15.1% to 80.1%), which outperforming most previously reported SBHs, supercapacitors and aqueous batteries (Table S2), especially bismuth-based devices, for instance, a MnO2//Bi2O3 asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) (∼85% after 4000 cycles) [35], a LiMn2O4//Bi2O3 battery (68.3% after 100 cycles) [36], a Co3O4/GN/BC//r-Bi2O3/GN/BC ASC (88% after 3000 cycles) [37], a NiCo2O4//Bi2O3 ASC (79% after 5000 cycles) [38], a NiCo2O4//Bi battery (89% after 1000 cycles) [41], a Ni(OH)2//(BiO)4CO3(OH)2 battery (∼60% after 200 cycles) [54], a AC//NiO-AC NFs SBH (88% after 5000 cycles) [55], a Ni-G-CNFs@PANI supercapacitor (71.8% after 2000 cycles) [56], a Ni(OH)2@Co9S8//FeS2/GNS ASC (86% after 5000 cycles) [57], and a NiCo2S4//rGO/Fe2O3 ASC (90% after 1000 cycles) [58]. This remarkable improvement should be attributed to the flexible confinement effect of few-layer graphene uniformly welded on nanoflakes. In addition, although CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-C has a slightly higher capacity retention due to the dense carbon layer (Fig. S15), its energy density is limited below CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G.

The schematic of Fig. 5 a envisages the mechanism of the graphene in-situ nano-welded structure for improving cyclic stability. Specifically, the following three main effects may be possible. (1) The presence of graphene and CDs can homogenize the current density, thus slows down the formation of bulk crystals. (2) The flexible and folded structure of graphene can eliminate the stress caused by the volume change of active material. (3) Even if dendrites are formed, it is impossible to pierce the graphene encapsulation layer with high modulus. In order to clarify the advantages and mechanism of in-situ nano-welded graphene, we characterized the devices and disassembled anodes after cycling stability test. The CV curves and EIS plots of these three devices are displayed in Fig. 5b and c. For CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3, the pseudocapacitance is almost vanishing and the impedance has seriously deteriorated, which means that the active materials perhaps aggregate into chunks and is detached from the substrate. But the devices assembled with modification electrodes maintain the initial CV profiles, the area of the loops are reduced to the same ratio as the capacity drop. Moreover, the straight line of CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G in the low frequency range becomes perpendicular to the real axis, which can be attributed to the loss of exposed active materials while those encapsulated in graphene with better capacitance characteristic remains intact. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of Fig. S16 reveal that the CNT@Bi2O3-G contains a mixture of various bismuth oxides after the cycle test (JCPDS 27-0049 and JCPDS 51-1161). And corresponding EDS results indicate the Bi2O3 content is ∼ 84.4 wt% (Fig. S17).

Fig. S18 shows the overall morphological changes of the CNT@Bi2O3-G anode compared with the CNT@Bi2O3 anode. It is obvious that there is no significant active materials agglomeration on CNT@Bi2O3-G, whereas many irregular bulks are stuck in CNTs for CNT@Bi2O3. For CNT@Bi2O3-C, although most of the active material is still encapsulated in the carbon shell, significant cracking of the carbon shell and agglomeration of some of the active material can also be clearly observed (Fig. S19). The detailed morphological changes are further displayed in the FESEM images of Fig. 5d–f. As we expected, the Bi2O3 of the raw anode has been completely detached from the carbon nanotubes and has been reunite into micron-sized blocks. Quite differently, with the help of the adaptable graphene layer, the CNT@Bi2O3-G maintains the original hierarchical structure and only slightly thins due to the slow loss of active materials. For CNT@Bi2O3-C, the calcined dense carbon layer can effectively restrict the active material, but the volumetric change in the charge-discharge process leads to slight breakage of carbon layer and the subsequent loss and reunion of active material. In addition, for CNT@Bi2O3-G, although some active materials would be lost during charge-discharge process, they would be re-deposited on graphene with a certain nanosheets structure rather than irregular bulks (Fig. S20), which confirms the hypothesis that graphene layer can homogenize the current density and make the active materials evenly deposit back.

In summary, we have developed a novel graphene in-situ nano-welded technique for CNT@Bi2O3 self-standing anodes. Because of the simple microwave irradiation process, the synthesis method is rapid and low energy consumption, and has the prospect of large-scale application. Benefiting from the unique graphene/CDs encapsulated hierarchical nanostructures, large aspect ratio and inherent high electrochemical activity of Bi2O3 nanoflakes, and intrinsically flexible of CNTs film substrate, a high capacity of 1.90 mAh cm−2/297.0 mAh g−1 at 1 mA cm−2 and superior rate performance (82.6% retention from 1 mA cm−2 to 100 mA cm−2) are afforded by the CNT@Bi2O3-G electrode. When the CNT@Bi2O3-G electrode is applied as a self-standing anode, the as-assembled CNT@NiCo2O4//CNT@Bi2O3-G SBH delivers an extraordinarily high energy density of 589.3 μWh cm−2/98.2 Wh kg−1 at the power density of 0.8 mW cm−2. Most notably, compared with a device assembled with a raw anode, the 8000th cycle stability of the device is increased by ∼4.5 times. All of the abovementioned advantages demonstrate that our ingenious strategy is promising in a wide range of nanoscale engineering.
